title: Illegitimate Court Counteraction Act
plain_summary: This bill punishes the International Criminal Court when it tries to investigate or arrest Americans or people from US ally countries that never agreed to let the court have power over them. If the court goes after these protected people, the President must block certain foreign individuals from entering the US and freeze their money and property. The bill also stops all US government money from going to the International Criminal Court. The punishments also apply to family members of people who help the court with these cases.
last_action_text: Cloture on the motion to proceed to the measure not invoked in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 54 - 45. Record Vote Number: 22. (CR S410)
subjects: Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Illegitimate Court Counteraction Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has decided these facts are true. The United States and Israel never agreed to join the International Criminal Court, so this court has no right to control or judge either country. On May twenty, two thousand twenty four, the court's prosecutor Karim Khan asked for arrest warrants against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defense Minister Yoav Gallant. Congress strongly disagrees with this action. On November twenty one, two thousand twenty four, the court actually issued these arrest warrants, which Congress also strongly disagrees with. In two thousand two, Congress passed a law called the American Servicemembers Protection Act to protect US military members and officials from being prosecuted by international courts that the US never joined. That law warned that the International Criminal Court could try to prosecute the President and other top US officials. The court's actions against Israel are wrong and create a dangerous example that threatens the United States, Israel, and all US partners who never agreed to let this court judge them. The United States must fight against any action by this court against the US, Israel, or any other ally that never agreed to the court's power or never joined the court's founding agreement called the Rome Statute.

sections.title: Section 3  Sanctions With Respect to the International Criminal Court
sections.text: Within sixty days after this law takes effect, and then regularly after that, if the International Criminal Court is trying to investigate, arrest, hold, or prosecute any protected person (a person covered by this law), the President must punish foreign people in two ways. First, the President must impose the punishments described in part b of this section on any foreign person who has directly helped the court investigate, arrest, hold, or prosecute a protected person, or who has given the court money, materials, technology, goods, or services to help with these actions, or who is owned or controlled by someone who helps the court with these actions. Second, the President must impose the visa ban punishment described in part b number two on the immediate family members of each foreign person being punished. The punishments are blocking all property and money of these foreign people if it is in the United States or comes under US control, and banning these people from getting visas or entering the United States. Any visas they already have will be cancelled immediately. The President can use all the powers from the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to carry out these punishments. Anyone who breaks these rules will face the same penalties as breaking that other law. Within ten days of imposing punishments, the President must tell Congress in writing about who is being punished, what they did to help the court, and what specific punishments were imposed. The President can waive these punishments for up to ninety days at a time if the President tells Congress in writing that the waiver is vital to US national security. The waiver report must explain why it is needed, what the person did wrong, what the US has done to stop the court, and what the court has done to end its investigations of protected persons. The President can end all punishments if the President certifies in writing to Congress that the court has stopped all efforts to investigate, arrest, hold, or prosecute protected persons and has permanently closed all investigations.

sections.title: Section 4  Rescission of Funds for International Criminal Court
sections.text: Starting when this law takes effect, any money that was set aside for the International Criminal Court and is still available to spend is now taken back. From this date forward, no government money can be used for the International Criminal Court.

sections.title: Section 5  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following. Admitted alien means the same thing as defined in the Immigration and Nationality Act. Ally of the United States means either a government that belongs to NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization) or a government that is a major non NATO ally as defined in the American Service Members Protection Act. Appropriate congressional committees means the House committees on Foreign Affairs, Financial Services, and Judiciary, and the Senate committees on Foreign Relations, Banking Housing and Urban Affairs, and Judiciary. Foreign person means any person who is not a United States person. Immediate family member means a spouse, parent, sibling, or adult child of a person. International Criminal Court and Rome Statute have the same meaning as in the American Service Members Protection Act. Protected person means any United States person unless the US formally agrees to the court's power and joins the Rome Statute, including current or former US military members, current or former elected or appointed US government officials, and any other person currently or formerly employed by or working for the US government. It also means any foreign person who is a citizen or legal resident of a US ally that has not agreed to the court's power or joined the Rome Statute, including current or former military members of that ally, current or former elected or appointed government officials of that ally, and any other person currently or formerly employed by or working for that government. United States person means an individual who is a US citizen or legal permanent resident, a business organized under US laws including foreign branches of such businesses, or any person in the United States.
